does: This paper systematically evaluates the reliability of automated judges used to score attack success rates in jailbreak and prompt injection research. The authors compare two judge families—dedicated safety classifiers and general LLMs prompted for grading—against 596 human-labeled completions from HarmBench's validation set. They find that dedicated classifiers exhibit high recall but low precision (0.835 precision, 0.974 recall), over-flagging harmful content, while LLM-as-judges maintain reasonable precision (0.81–0.94) but show highly erratic recall (0.06–0.65), causing the same responses to receive dramatically different harm assessments depending on which judge scores them. The authors further demonstrate that both judge families are vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, with benign wrapper text and prepended refusal sentences flipping LLM judge verdicts 39–100% of the time, though dedicated classifiers prove substantially more robust to surface-level attacks.
connects: This work directly addresses a foundational problem in recent LLM safety research: the reliability of evaluation metrics themselves. It builds on growing awareness in the community that automated evaluation of harm and safety remains inadequately validated, extending prior work on prompt injection and jailbreak defenses by questioning the measurement apparatus rather than the attacks or defenses. The paper connects to broader literature on classifier calibration, adversarial robustness of classifiers, and the known gap between automated metrics and human judgment in NLP tasks. It responds to the implicit assumption in dozens of recent papers that attack-success rates computed by off-the-shelf judges accurately reflect true harm, a problem recognized but not systematically quantified in earlier safety evaluations.
enables: This work opens several important research directions in LLM safety evaluation. It establishes that reliable harm judgment requires either better calibration of existing classifiers, ensemble methods combining judge families, or human-in-the-loop validation for critical safety claims. The demonstrated adversarial vulnerability of LLM judges motivates future work on robust evaluation protocols and suggests that papers claiming high attack success rates against LLM-based judges may be gaming unreliable metrics. The findings call for standardization of safety evaluation practices, development of judge-agnostic evaluation frameworks, and deeper investigation into why different judges fail in opposite ways. This work also enables meta-research into the trustworthiness of empirical claims in jailbreak literature, potentially reshaping how the community validates safety interventions and interprets published attack success rates.
